docs.title: Karva (0.1.8)
docs.text: A Python test framework, written in Rust.

docs.title: About Karva
docs.text:
Karva aims to be an efficient alternative to pytest and unittest.

While we do not yet support all of pytest's features, we aim to gradually add support for pytest alternatives as we add features.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Karva is available as karva on PyPI.

Use karva directly with uvx:

uvx karva test
uvx karva version

Or install karva with uv, or pip:

# With uv.
uv tool install karva@latest

# Add karva to your project.
uv add --dev karva

# With pip.
pip install karva

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
By default, Karva will respect your .gitignore files when discovering tests in specified directories.

To run your tests, try any of the following:

# Run all tests.
karva test

# Run tests in a specific directory.
karva test tests/

# Run tests in a specific file.
karva test tests/test_example.py

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Here is a small example usage

tests/test.py 

def test_pass():
    assert True


def test_fail():
    assert False, "This test should fail"


def test_error():
    raise ValueError("This is an error")

Running karva:

uv run karva test tests/test.py

Provides the following output:

test tests.test::test_pass ... ok
test tests.test::test_fail ... FAILED
test tests.test::test_error ... FAILED

fail[assertion-error]: This test should fail
 --> tests.test::test_fail at tests/test.py:5
 | File "tests/test.py", line 6, in test_fail
 |   assert False, "This test should fail"

fail[value-error]: This is an error
 --> tests.test::test_error at tests/test.py:9
 | File "tests/test.py", line 10, in test_error
 |   raise ValueError("This is an error")

test result: FAILED. 1 passed; 2 failed; 0 skipped; finished in 0s

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Contributions are welcome! See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information.

docs.title: karva
docs.text:
A Python test runner.

Usage 
karva <COMMAND>
Commands karva test

Run tests

karva version

Display Karva's version

karva help

Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

docs.title: karva test
docs.text:
Run tests

Usage 
karva test [OPTIONS] [PATH]...
Arguments PATHS

List of files, directories, or test functions to test [default: the project root]

Options --help, -h

Print help

--no-ignore

When set, .gitignore files will not be respected.

--quiet, -q

Use quiet output (or -qq for silent output)

--show-output, -s

Show Python stdout during test execution

--test-prefix test-prefix

The prefix of the test functions

[default: test]

--verbose, -v

Use verbose output (or -vv and -vvv for more verbose output)

docs.title: karva version
docs.text:
Display Karva's version

Usage 
karva version
Options --help, -h

Print help

docs.title: karva help
docs.text:
Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Usage 
karva help [COMMAND]

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text: We only discover package level fixtures from a conftest.py file in the root of that package.

docs.title: Fixture Types
docs.text: Karva supports different types of fixtures based on their scope:

docs.title: Function Scope
docs.text: The default scope. The fixture is created for each test function.

docs.title: Module Scope
docs.text: The fixture is created once per test module.

docs.title: Package Scope
docs.text:
The fixture is created once per test package.

This means that every package that is a sub-package of the package with the fixture will create the fixture if and only if it has a module that requires the fixture.

For example, if we have the following structure:

calculator
├── src
└── tests
    ├── conftest.py
    ├── bar
    │   └── test_bar.py
    └── foo
        └── test_foo.py

If we have the following fixture in tests/conftest.py:

@fixture(scope="package")
def package_fixture():
    return "package"

And if the fixtures is used in tests/foo/test_foo.py and tests/bar/test_bar.py then fixture will be created once for the foo package and once for the bar package.

If you wanted it to only create the fixture once you should use the session scope.

docs.title: Session Scope
docs.text: The fixture is created once per test session.

docs.title: Dynamic Scope
docs.text:
A dynamic scope is given as a function that returns a valid scope string.

def dynamic_scope(fixture_name, config):
    return "module"

Currently, we do not support config and that value is passed as None. The fixture_name argument is a string.

docs.title: Dependent fixtures
docs.text:
We support fixtures that depend on other fixtures.

from karva import fixture

@fixture
def function_fixture():
    return "function"

@fixture
def dependent_fixture(function_fixture: str) -> str:
    return function_fixture + "dependent"

def test_dependent(dependent_fixture: str):
    assert dependent_fixture == "functiondependent"

docs.title: Finalizers
docs.text:
We support finalizers. These are called after the scope of the fixture has finished running.

from karva import fixture

@fixture
def finalizer_fixture():
    print("Finalizer fixture initialized")
    yield 1
    print("Finalizer fixture finalizer called")

def test_finalizer(finalizer_fixture: int):
    assert finalizer_fixture == 1

docs.title: Auto-use fixtures
docs.text:
We support auto-use fixtures. These are fixtures that are automatically used in their scope.

from karva import fixture

data = {}

@fixture(auto_use=True)
def add_data():
    data.update(value=True)

def test_value():
    assert data.get('value')

docs.title: Other ways to use fixtures
docs.text:
Seen here in pytest.

You can wrap your test function with a decorator specifying what fixtures you would like to call before running the function.

This is technically a tag, but we reference it here has it refers to fixtures.

import karva

@karva.fixture
def x():
    # Do something
    return ...

@karva.fixture
def y():
    # Do something
    yield ...
    # Do something else

@karva.tags.use_fixtures("x", "y")
def test():
    # Do something

docs.title: Overriding fixtures
docs.text:
We can override fixtures by giving them the same name. When overriding them we can also use them as arguments.

conftest.py

import pytest

@pytest.fixture
def username():
    return 'username'

test_something.py

def test_username(username):
    assert username == 'username'

subfolder/conftest.py

import pytest

@pytest.fixture
def username(username):
    return 'overridden-' + username

subfolder/test_something_else.py

def test_username(username):
    assert username == 'overridden-username'

docs.title: Parametrizing fixtures
docs.text:
You can parametrize fixtures by using the @karva.fixture(params=...) decorator.

import karva

@karva.fixture(params=['username', 'email'])
def some_fixture(request):
    return request.param

def test_username_email(some_fixture):
    assert some_fixture in ['username', 'email']

This will run test_username_email twice, once with username and once with email.

Here we also see that we can "introspect" the fixture by using the request object.

Currently the only parameter you can use here is request.param.

In future you will be able to access other parameters.

It is important to note that this request object is not the same as the pytest FixtureRequest object. It is a custom object provided by Karva. And so it may not have all of the information that the pytest FixtureRequest object has.

docs.title: Fixture scopes with parametrized fixtures
docs.text:
See this example:

import karva

@karva.fixture(params=['username', 'email'])
def some_fixture(request):
    return request.param

@karva.fixture(scope="function")
def another_fixture():
    return "another_fixture"

def test_username_email(some_fixture, another_fixture):
    assert some_fixture in ['username', 'email']

Our current implementation means that we will only run another_fixture once.

While this may not be ideal for all scenarios, it is a trade-off that we have made to ensure that our fixtures are efficient and performant.

docs.title: Built-in fixtures
docs.text: Karva provides a few built-in fixtures that can be used in your tests.

docs.title: Temporary Directory
docs.text:
This fixture provides the user with a pathlib.Path object that points to a temporary directory.

You can use any of the following fixture names to use this fixture:

  • tmp_path # from pytest
  • temp_path # from karva
  • temp_dir # from karva

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Karva is available as karva on PyPI.

Use karva directly with uvx:

uvx karva test
uvx karva version

Or install karva with uv, or pip:

# With uv.
uv tool install karva@latest

# Add karva to your project.
uv add --dev karva

# With pip.
pip install karva

Once installed, you can use karva to run your tests:

karva test

Or to get the version of karva you're using:

karva version

docs.title: Tutorial
docs.text: This tutorial will walk you through the basics of using Karva.

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
We will first create a new project using uv.

uv init --lib calculator

This will give us a project that looks like this:

calculator
├── pyproject.toml
├── README.md
└── src
    └── calculator
        ├── __init__.py
        └── py.typed

We can then create our core logic in src/calculator/__init__.py.

class Calculator:
    def add(self, a: int, b: int) -> int:
        return a + b

We can then create our tests in tests/test_add.py.

from calculator import Calculator

def test_add():
    calculator = Calculator()
    assert calculator.add(1, 2) == 3

Then, we'll add karva to our project.

uv add --dev karva

We can then run our tests with uv run karva test.

uv run karva test

docs.title: Parametrize
docs.text:
This works like pytest's parametrize decorator.

Note

When mixing fixtures and parametrize, the parametrize values take priority.

First, here is a small example:

import karva

@karva.tags.parametrize("a", [1, 2, 3])
def test_function(a: int):
    assert a > 0

Then running uv run karva test will provide the following output:

test test::test_function [a=1] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=2] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=3] ... ok

test result: ok. 3 passed; 0 failed; 0 skipped; finished in 0s

We can also parametrize multiple arguments:

import karva

@karva.tags.parametrize(("a", "b"), [(1, 4), (2, 5), (3, 6)])
def test_function(a: int, b: int):
    assert a > 0 and b > 0

Then running uv run karva test will provide the following output:

test test::test_function [a=1, b=4] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=2, b=5] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=3, b=6] ... ok

test result: ok. 3 passed; 0 failed; 0 skipped; finished in 0s

We can also mix fixtures and parametrize:

import karva

@karva.fixture
def b() -> int:
    return 1

@karva.tags.parametrize("a", [1, 2])
def test_function(a: int, b: int):
    assert a > 0 and b > 0

Then running uv run karva test -v will provide the following output:

test test::test_function [a=1, b=1] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=2, b=1] ... ok

test result: ok. 2 passed; 0 failed; 0 skipped; finished in 0s

We can also use multiple decorators:

This will result in a sort of cartesian product of the parametrize values.

import karva

@karva.tags.parametrize("a", [1, 2])
@karva.tags.parametrize("b", [1, 2])
def test_function(a: int, b: int):
    assert a > 0 and b > 0

Then running uv run karva test -v will provide the following output:

test test::test_function [a=1, b=1] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=2, b=1] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=1, b=2] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=2, b=2] ... ok

test result: ok. 4 passed; 0 failed; 0 skipped; finished in 0s

We can also still use pytest's parametrize decorator:

import pytest

@pytest.mark.parametrize("a", [1, 2])
def test_function(a: int):
    assert a > 0

Then running uv run karva test -v will provide the following output:

test test::test_function [a=1] ... ok
test test::test_function [a=2] ... ok

test result: ok. 2 passed; 0 failed; 0 skipped; finished in 0s

docs.title: Skip Tag
docs.text: The skip tag allows you to mark test functions to be skipped during test execution. When a test is skipped, it will not be run but will be counted in the test results.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Skip without reason

import karva

@karva.tags.skip
def test_example():
    assert False  # This will not be executed

Skip with function call

import karva

@karva.tags.skip()
def test_example():
    assert False  # This will not be executed

Skip with reason (positional argument)

import karva

@karva.tags.skip("This test is not implemented yet")
def test_example():
    assert False  # This will not be executed

Skip with reason (keyword argument)

import karva

@karva.tags.skip(reason="Waiting for feature X to be implemented")
def test_example():
    assert False  # This will not be executed

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
When a test is marked with the skip tag:

  1. The test function will not be executed
  2. The test will be counted as "skipped" in the test results
  3. If a reason is provided, it will be shown in the info logs (access via using -v)

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Temporarily disable tests: When debugging or working on specific functionality
  • Mark incomplete tests: Tests that are written but not yet ready to run
  • Platform-specific tests: Tests that should only run on certain platforms
  • Feature flags: Tests for features that are not yet enabled

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
When running tests with skipped test cases:

test result: ok. 2 passed; 0 failed; 1 skipped

The skipped tests contribute to the total count but do not cause the test suite to fail.
